metadata.title: Guideline on clinical investigation of medicinal products for prevention of stroke and systemic embolic events in patients with non-valvular atrial fibrillation

rules.rule_text: The composite primary efficacy endpoint must be time to first stroke (including ischaemic and undefined strokes) and systemic embolic events (SEEs) from randomisation.
rules.verbatim_quote: The composite primary efficacy endpoint of time to first stroke (including ischaemic and undefined strokes) and SEEs from randomisation is therefore recommended.

rules.rule_text: A mandatory secondary analysis must include separate reporting of individual components of the primary endpoint: ischaemic strokes, undefined strokes, and non-central nervous embolic events.
rules.verbatim_quote: A mandatory secondary analysis should include separately the individual components of the recommended primary efficacy endpoint i.e. ischaemic and undefined strokes, and other non-central nervous embolic events.

rules.rule_text: A net clinical benefit secondary endpoint combining all strokes (ischaemic, undefined, haemorrhagic) and other non-central nervous system embolic events is recommended.
rules.verbatim_quote: A clinically relevant net clinical benefit secondary endpoint consisting of "all strokes (i.e. ischaemic, undefined and haemorrhagic stroke) and other non-central nervous system embolic events" is therefore recommended.
rules.exceptions: All major bleedings, all-cause death or vascular death may also be acceptable for inclusion as part of a net clinical benefit secondary endpoint.

rules.rule_text: Stroke must be defined by a generally accepted definition (SDCCT Initiative or WHO definition) and all efforts must classify strokes as primary ischaemic or primary haemorrhagic.
rules.verbatim_quote: Stroke should be defined by a generally accepted definition [i.e. Standardized Data Collection for Cardiovascular Trials (SDCCT Initiative) definition; World Health Organisation (WHO) definition]. All efforts should be made to classify strokes as "primary ischaemic" (component of the primary endpoint) or "primary haemorrhagic" (not a component of the primary endpoint).

rules.rule_text: An ischaemic stroke with haemorrhagic conversion must be classified as primary ischaemic, not haemorrhagic.
rules.verbatim_quote: An ischaemic stroke with haemorrhagic conversion should be considered as "primary ischaemic".

rules.rule_text: Stroke subtype classification must be based on clinical symptoms and neuroimaging results (CT and/or MRI) and/or autopsy.
rules.verbatim_quote: It is therefore recommended that the classification of stroke subtype is based on clinical symptoms and results from neuroimaging (computed tomographic and/or magnetic resonance scanning) and/or autopsy.

rules.rule_text: Intracranial haemorrhages (haemorrhagic stroke, subdural or epidural haematoma, subarachnoid haemorrhage) must not be part of the primary efficacy outcome and must be assessed as a safety endpoint (major bleedings).
rules.verbatim_quote: Intracranial haemorrhages (i.e.: haemorrhagic stroke/intracerebral haemorrhage, subdural or epidural haematoma/haemorrhage, or subarachnoid haemorrhage) should not be part of the primary efficacy outcome and should be primarily assessed as safety endpoint (major bleedings).

rules.rule_text: A suspected TIA must be adjudicated as stroke if positive neuroimaging confirms a cerebral infarction, even if symptom duration is less than 24 hours.
rules.verbatim_quote: A suspected TIA should be adjudicated as stroke if there is positive neuroimaging confirming a cerebral infarction, even if the duration of symptoms is of less than 24 hours.

rules.rule_text: The occurrence and classification of primary endpoint components must be adjudicated by an independent and blinded committee.
rules.verbatim_quote: The occurrence and classification of the components of the primary endpoint should be adjudicated by an independent and blinded committee in order to limit the introduction of bias caused by differences in diagnostic sensitivity and local standards of care.

rules.rule_text: The diagnosis of SEEs must be confirmed by findings from angiography, surgery, scintigraphy, and/or autopsy, and the location of vascular occlusion must be specified.
rules.verbatim_quote: The diagnosis of SEEs should be defined by a generally accepted definition. The diagnosis should be confirmed by findings from angiography, surgery, scintigraphy, and/or autopsy. The location of the vascular occlusion should also be specified.

rules.rule_text: Final stroke outcome must be assessed at 3-6 months after stroke onset using a validated stroke outcome scale, preferably the modified Rankin scale.
rules.verbatim_quote: Final stroke outcome should be assessed at 3-6 months after stroke onset using a validated stroke outcome scale, preferably the widely used modified Rankin scale.
rules.exceptions: Other validated stroke outcome scales (e.g. Barthel Index) could be used in sensitivity analyses.

rules.rule_text: Disabling stroke is defined as modified Rankin scale 3-5; non-disabling stroke is defined as modified Rankin scale 0-2.
rules.verbatim_quote: A disabling stroke should be defined as a score on the modified Rankin scale of 3-5, whereas a non-disabling stroke should be defined as a score of 0-2.

rules.rule_text: All secondary efficacy endpoints must be adjudicated by an independent and blinded committee.
rules.verbatim_quote: All secondary efficacy endpoints should be adjudicated by an independent and blinded committee in order to limit the introduction of any bias.

rules.rule_text: Patients must have documented NVAF (atrial fibrillation with absence of haemodynamically significant valvular disease or prosthetic valve), confirmed on two separate occasions by ECG, Holter monitoring, pacemaker, or cardiac defibrillator read outs.
rules.verbatim_quote: Patients to be included should have NVAF (i.e. with documentation of both atrial fibrillation and absence of haemodynamically significant valvular disease or prosthetic valve). AF may be paroxysmal, persistent or permanent, but not secondary to a reversible disorder such as myocardial infarction, pulmonary embolism, recent surgery, pericarditis or thyrotoxicosis. AF has to be documented on two separate occasions by ECG evidence, Holter monitoring, pacemaker or cardiac defibrillator read outs.
rules.exceptions: AF must not be secondary to reversible disorders (MI, PE, recent surgery, pericarditis, thyrotoxicosis).

rules.rule_text: Patients must present with a level of thrombo-embolic risk justifying anticoagulant therapy as recommended by current guidelines. CHADS2 score must be included in patient categorisation.
rules.verbatim_quote: Patients should present at inclusion with a level of thrombo-embolic risk justifying anticoagulant therapy, as recommended by current guidelines. CHADS2 score [1] should be included in the categorisation and description of the patient population.

rules.rule_text: If VKA-naive patients are included, VKA naive must be defined as VKA use for less than 6 weeks immediately before trial entry.
rules.verbatim_quote: if the clinical trial is intended to include VKA-naive and VKA-experienced patients, VKA naive may be defined as VKA use for < 6 weeks immediately before entry into the trial [9].
rules.exceptions: Additional accepted definitions may be used as sensitivity analysis (patients not on VKA at randomization; patients who had never been on VKA; patients who previously had received a total of <= 2 months of VKA therapy).

rules.rule_text: PK trials must be performed in healthy volunteers and patients. PK profile must also be studied in patients with impaired renal function, impaired liver function, extreme body-weights, and older patients.
rules.verbatim_quote: Pharmacokinetics trials should be performed in healthy volunteers and in patients in order to obtain information on the absorption, distribution, metabolism and excretion of the product following its proposed route of administration. In addition, pharmacokinetic profile of the product in development should also be studied in the following specific patient populations: patients with impaired renal function, impaired liver function, extreme body-weights, and older patients.

rules.rule_text: All potential clinically relevant drug-drug or drug-food interactions must be specifically investigated before approval.
rules.verbatim_quote: All potential clinically relevant drug-drug or drug-food interactions derived from the pharmacokinetic or pharmacodynamic characteristics of the investigational medicinal product should be specifically investigated, preferentially before approval.

rules.rule_text: Confirmatory trials must use a prospective, double-blind, randomised, controlled, parallel group clinical trial design.
rules.verbatim_quote: The most appropriate design for confirmatory trials is considered to be a prospective, double-blind randomized, controlled, parallel group clinical trial.
rules.exceptions: Data from open label studies using VKA as comparator might be acceptable if outcomes are blindly adjudicated, methodology is robust, and results are clinically and statistically meaningful.

rules.rule_text: An active control group is normally required in pivotal studies. VKA or direct oral anticoagulants (oral direct thrombin inhibitor, oral direct FXa inhibitor) are valid comparators.
rules.source_section: 7.4 Therapeutic studies - Choice of control group
rules.verbatim_quote: An active control group is normally required in pivotal studies due to the severity of the disease to be prevented (stroke and/or SEE). VKA or direct oral anticoagulants approved in this indication (e.g.: oral direct thrombin inhibitor, oral direct FXa inhibitor) are considered valid comparators in this clinical setting.
rules.exceptions: Use of placebo may be appropriate when the new antithrombotic is given on top of standard of care, or in patients at apparently low risk of thromboembolism (CHADS2 score = 0).

rules.rule_text: ASA must not be used as control in patients with CHADS2 score >= 2 due to its poorer efficacy compared to VKA.
rules.source_section: 7.4 Therapeutic studies - Choice of control group
rules.verbatim_quote: The use of ASA as control is discouraged in patients with a CHADS2 score of >= 2 due to its poorer efficacy in comparison to VKA.

rules.rule_text: The protocol must describe management of anticoagulant therapy during cardioversion, catheter ablation, elective and urgent surgical procedures, and major trauma.
rules.verbatim_quote: Concomitant procedures: the protocol has to describe the management of anticoagulant therapy during the clinical trial in case of cardioversion, catheter ablation, elective and urgent surgical procedures as well as major trauma.

rules.rule_text: When VKA is used as comparator, quality of oral anticoagulation must be measured by time in therapeutic range (TTR) calculated by the Rosendaal method, including total time on and off drug.
rules.source_section: 7.4 Therapeutic studies - Quality of oral anticoagulation
rules.verbatim_quote: When VKA is used as comparator, the quality of oral anticoagulation should be based on the time in therapeutic range (TTR) calculated by the Rosendaal method [11]. The calculation of the TTR should include the total time on and off drug in all patients.

rules.rule_text: The impact of quality of oral anticoagulation on main efficacy and safety outcomes must be shown by quartiles of center TTR (cTTR) and by cTTR intervals (<50%, 50-65%, >65%).
rules.source_section: 7.4 Therapeutic studies - Quality of oral anticoagulation
rules.verbatim_quote: The impact of quality of oral anticoagulation on the main efficacy and safety outcomes has to be shown: - By quartiles of center time in therapeutic range (cTTR): below 1st quartile, between 1st and 2nd quartile, between 2nd and 3rd quartile, above 3rd quartile. - By cTTR, in the following intervals of cTTR: <50%, 50-65%; >65%.

rules.rule_text: A non-inferiority approach (followed or not by hierarchical superiority) is recommended in actively controlled trials; superiority is mandatory in placebo-controlled trials.
rules.verbatim_quote: A non-inferiority approach (followed or not by hierarchical superiority) is recommended in actively controlled trials, while superiority approach is mandatory in placebo-controlled trials.

rules.rule_text: The non-inferiority analysis must include primary endpoint events while taking study drug plus a 3-day period after discontinuation (on-treatment analysis). Sensitivity analyses must include events occurring 1 week and 1 month after discontinuation.
rules.verbatim_quote: The analysis to show non-inferiority should include the primary endpoint events while taking study drug including a period of 3 days after study drug discontinuation (on-treatment analysis). Sensitivity analyses should include events occurring 1 week and 1 month after study drug discontinuation in order to investigate a possible early rebound increase in thromboembolism after treatment cessation.

rules.rule_text: The superiority analysis must include all primary endpoint events from randomisation through end of study.
rules.verbatim_quote: The analysis to show superiority should include all primary endpoint events occurring through end of study (from each patient's date of randomization to the estimated date of attainment of the study's target of primary endpoint events).

rules.rule_text: The study must include a follow-up of at least 30 days after last day of study drug, and a plan for safely transitioning subjects off study medication at study termination.
rules.verbatim_quote: The study should include a follow-up of at least 30 days after last day of study drug, and a plan for safely transitioning subjects off of study medication at study termination should be properly addressed.

rules.rule_text: The primary safety endpoint in pivotal trials must be major bleeding. The sum of major and clinically relevant non-major bleeding must be analysed as a secondary safety endpoint.
rules.verbatim_quote: In pivotal trials, the recommended primary safety endpoint is major bleeding, but the sum of major and clinically relevant non-major bleeding is to be analysed as well (secondary endpoint).
rules.exceptions: In dose-finding studies, sum of major and clinically relevant non-major bleeding is the recommended sensitive safety endpoint.

rules.rule_text: Major bleeding is defined as: fatal bleeding; critical organ bleeding (intracranial, intraocular, intraspinal, pericardial, retroperitoneal, intraarticular, or intramuscular with compartment syndrome); clinically overt bleeding with haemoglobin decrease >2 g/dL; transfusion of >=2 units of blood or packed cells; or clinically overt bleeding necessitating surgical intervention.
rules.verbatim_quote: Major bleeding is defined as a bleeding event that meets at least one of the following criteria: - fatal bleeding - critical bleeding (intracranial, intraocular, intraspinal, pericardial, retroperitoneal, intraarticular or intramuscular with compartment syndrome) - clinically overt bleeding associated with a decrease in the haemoglobin level of more than 2 g/dL (20 g/l; 1.24 mmol/L) compared with the pre-randomisation level - clinically overt bleeding leading to transfusion of two or more units of whole blood or packed cells - clinically overt bleeding that necessitates surgical intervention

rules.rule_text: Major bleedings must be described by localisation (intracranial vs. extracranial) and outcome (death, disability, recovered without sequels).
rules.verbatim_quote: major bleedings should also be described by localisation (e.g.: intracranial and extra-cranial, separately) and outcome (e.g.: resulting in death; resulting in disability; recovered without sequels).

rules.rule_text: The adjudication of bleeding events by a central independent and blinded committee of experts, using pre-specified limits and clear terms of reference, is strongly encouraged.
rules.verbatim_quote: Similar to the efficacy evaluation, the adjudication of bleeding events by a central independent and blinded committee of experts, using pre-specified limits and clear terms of reference is strongly encouraged.

rules.rule_text: Development of a specific antidote or reversal agent studies for the new anticoagulant is highly recommended for long-term use in stroke prevention in AF.
rules.source_section: 8.1 Bleeding events - The need for reversal and laboratory monitoring
rules.verbatim_quote: The development of a specific antidote or further specific studies with non-specific reversal agent for new antithrombotics when given at high doses for long-term, as in stroke prevention in AF, is highly recommended given the potential for life-threatening bleeding events in standard practice.

rules.rule_text: Development of a standardised laboratory test for monitoring the anticoagulant effect of new agents is highly recommended.
rules.source_section: 8.1 Bleeding events - The need for reversal and laboratory monitoring
rules.verbatim_quote: The development of a standardised test for laboratory monitoring of the anticoagulant effect of new agents is highly recommended.

rules.rule_text: Geriatric patients >65 years and >=75 years must be appropriately represented in clinical trials. Clinical data in older (>=75) and frail oldest older persons (>=85 years) with high comorbidity is of utmost importance.
rules.verbatim_quote: to assess the benefit/risk balance of a drug that will be used in the geriatric population, patients >65 years and >=75 years should be appropriately represented in clinical trials (ICH E7). ... Generating clinical data in older (>=75) and frail oldest older persons (>=85 years) patients with high comorbidity is a matter of utmost importance.
